replies.text: ........and I just processed the statement that the number one isn't a prime. All other primes are divisible by two numbers and one is only divisible by itself . Is this semantics or is it mathematics? Shouldn't the number one be actually considered as a hyper prime if it isn't divisible by any other number except for itself?  and who says Pluto isn't a planet anymore? The current rules of English language is based on a democratic concept.........if majority of people use a word wrongly ,then that incorrect usage becomes correct usage , French is just a touch different. I'll have to hit a dope smoker with this one ; I don't myself partake. I realised early on that two was a prime number which many others including some high school teachers had overlooked , but that number one is a revelation.

author: @sceptic33
text: have you seen the Templar cross prime number layout? quite interesting I thought...  if all numbers are laid out on a spiral with 24 spokes, and the Templar cross drawn on top, all the primes are on the 8 lines forming the cross... although all the numbers on those lines aren't primes, so it's not much use fo predicting the next on really...

author: @stevesimpson5994
text: nice generative proof...  now to muddy the waters.  Does there exist a natural number that is not a product of primes and also not divisible by itself?  Yes, it's a "stupid question", but I'm inclined to ask them sometimes to see if we can learn anything new  :-)

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Steve Simpson if it fulfilled these conditions we wouldn't be able to call it a natural number, and it would be difficult to see how it could be called a number at all, but it is worth observing in the past that such thought experiments are how we ended up with things like imaginary numbers!

replies.author: @stevesimpson5994
replies.text:
Ashley Mills I was being a bit cheeky with this one.  Yes, impossible for natural numbers, but for other things..


Consider what division might have meant to say a cobbler.  Shoes are, for the 99th percentile of the population, things that come in pairs.  And not just any pairs mind, pairs that are sized the same..


So a cobbler who bequeaths his store of 9 shoes to his 3 sons will still have difficulty resolving the division, ie. in his special circumstances, 9 is not divisible amongst (by) 3.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Hehe good point. In the mathematical universe if the cobbler things are strange indeed :)

author: @eamonn280958
text: Not sure if you can use the formuld to n and then and say it works for any set because it works for your small set. I'm not saying your wrong but I don't get your proof. It seems like a maths trick rather than a proof. Although I could be wrong.

replies.author: @AnonCh4r1i
replies.text: eamonn280958 If you find a way to predict primes, please inbox me first, I will make us both extremely wealthy

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: eamonn280958 hi mate, the crux of the argument is that we show the reasoning step for any n, this allows us then to say, ok set n to be equal to the first small set where we can show explicitly that the claim holds, which means we know it holds for the n+1 th number. Then we define the set to be this new set, and set n to be the end of it, thus the set expands by 1 each iteration and ad infinitum expands to the whole set of natural numbers > 1

replies.author: @jorgenitales4128
replies.text: eamonn280958 It's the principle of mathematical induction. Prove that a property holds for the first number (1), suppose it holds for the nth number, and show that supposing this implies that it holds for the next number (n + 1).  This means that the property holds for all natural numbers, since 1 implies 2, 2 implies 3, ...
